fuzzy_prd: We need a backend-agnostic file storage toolkit combined with a small library of standalone data-shaping helpers. The storage side lets the application save, retrieve, and manage files through a single consistent interface regardless of where the bytes physically live — a local machine today, a remote object store tomorrow. A central registry component reads a configuration that names one or more storage destinations, lazily creates each destination on first use, caches it, and allows new storage backend types to be registered or new destinations added at runtime. Two safety concerns are non-negotiable: file access must always stay within the configured storage area so no caller can escape it, and failures must surface as well-structured, machine-readable errors carrying a stable category label, a domain error code, an HTTP-style status number, and a human-readable explanation — never raw system fault messages. Separately, a collection of small transformation utilities handles common recurring needs: abbreviating large counts for display, coercing scalar-or-list values into lists, converting words between singular and plural forms, generating short non-cryptographic fingerprints, reordering list elements, recursively transforming nested data objects, classifying data collection definitions, resolving the endpoint path for a named collection (including the system-reserved prefix logic), determining the relationship direction for a given viewpoint, recognizing special placeholder variables that begin with reserved tokens like the current-time marker, and parsing structured text safely to strip prototype-pollution attacks. All of this lives in the drive package and the shared utilities module.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: export default abstract class Storage { append(_location: string, _content: Buffer | string): Promise<Response>; copy(_src: string, _dest: string): Promise<Response>; delete(_location: string): Promise<DeleteResponse>; public driver(): unknown; exists(_location: string): Promise<ExistsResponse>; get(_location: string, _encoding?: string): Promise<ContentResponse<string>>; getBuffer(_location: string): Promise<ContentResponse<Buffer>>; getSignedUrl(_location: string, _options?: SignedUrlOptions): Promise<SignedUrlResponse>; getStat(_location: string): Promise<StatResponse>; getStream(_location: string, _range?: Range): NodeJS.ReadableStream; getUrl(_location: string): string; move(_src: string, _dest: string): Promise<Response>; put(_location: string, _content: Buffer | NodeJS.ReadableStream | string): Promise<Response>; prepend(_location: string, _content: Buffer | string): Promise<Response>; flatList(_prefix?: string): AsyncIterable<FileListResponse>; }

trigger_keywords: what error format does unsupported operation produce
what does method_not_supported error look like
what is the output when a backend rejects an operation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
Exact output format for unsupported operations: 'error=method_not_supported
code=E_METHOD_NOT_SUPPORTED
status=500
detail=Method <operation> is not supported for the driver <backendName>
'. The detail line includes both the operation name and the backend class name. Status is always 500. Code is always E_METHOD_NOT_SUPPORTED.

trigger_keywords: what error is thrown when no default disk is configured
what happens when manager config has no default
missing default disk name error format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
When no default disk name is configured: 'error=invalid_config
code=E_INVALID_CONFIG
status=500
detail=Make sure to define a default disk name inside config file
'. The exact detail message is 'Make sure to define a default disk name inside config file'.

trigger_keywords: what error when driver is not registered
unregistered backend kind error format
driver not supported error output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
When a disk references an unregistered driver: 'error=driver_not_supported
status=400
driver=<driverName>
detail=Driver <driverName> is not supported
'. Note: no 'code=' line is emitted (DriverNotSupported has no stable code in E_ format). Status is 400, not 500. The driver name is echoed on its own 'driver=' line.

trigger_keywords: what error when disk already exists
duplicate disk name error
adding a disk that is already defined
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
When adding a disk under a name that already exists: 'error=invalid_config
code=E_INVALID_CONFIG
status=500
detail=A disk named <name> is already defined
'. Exact detail template: 'A disk named <diskname> is already defined'.

trigger_keywords: what error when file does not exist
file not found error format
reading a missing file error output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
When reading a file that does not exist: 'error=file_not_found
code=E_FILE_NOT_FOUND
status=500
path=<location>
'. The filesystem error uses 'path=' instead of 'detail=' to avoid leaking OS error messages. No OS error text appears in the output.

trigger_keywords: what are the dynamic variable prefixes
which strings are recognized as dynamic variables
isDynamicVariable recognized tokens
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The recognized dynamic variable prefixes are exactly: ['$NOW', '$CURRENT_USER', '$CURRENT_ROLE']. A value is dynamic only if it is a string AND starts with one of these three prefixes. Non-string values always return false. Any other string (e.g. '$CURRENT_DATE', 'regular') returns false.

trigger_keywords: what is the system collection prefix
how does endpoint_for_collection handle system collections
what prefix identifies a system collection
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: System collections are identified by the prefix 'directus_'. For a collection named 'directus_<suffix>', the endpoint is '/<suffix>' (the 9 characters of 'directus_' are stripped). For any other collection name, the endpoint is '/items/<collectionName>'. Example: 'directus_users' -> '/users'; 'articles' -> '/items/articles'.

trigger_keywords: how does path containment work for absolute paths
what happens to leading slash in local storage path
how are parent directory traversal paths handled
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Path containment in LocalFileSystemStorage uses join(sep, relativePath) so: (1) A leading '/' is treated as relative to the root, not the filesystem root. (2) Leading '../../../' segments that would climb above root are clamped to root. (3) Parent-directory segments that stay within root are honored. The technique is: fullPath = join(root, join(sep, relativePath)).

trigger_keywords: how does abbreviate_number handle numbers below 1000
what is the default unit list for number abbreviation
what suffix is used for thousands millions billions trillions
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Numbers below 1000 are returned unchanged as their plain string form. Default units list is ['K', 'M', 'B', 'T']. Default decimalPlaces is 0. Negative numbers keep their sign prepended. Rounding that pushes a value up to the next magnitude rolls over to the next unit (e.g. rounding 999.5K to 1000K becomes 1M).

trigger_keywords: what is the simple hash output for hello
what hash algorithm is used for getSimpleHash
simple hash collision rate and output format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: getSimpleHash('hello') = '5e918d2'. The algorithm is non-cryptographic: hash = 0, then for each character: hash = 31 * hash + charCodeAt(i), with hash &= hash applied each iteration (bitwise AND for 32-bit truncation). Result is Math.abs(hash).toString(16) — lowercase hexadecimal string.

trigger_keywords: how does registry introspection report disk lists
what format is used for listDisks output
how are instantiated disks reported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: listDisks emits 'disks=<comma-separated-sorted-list>'. The list contains only INSTANTIATED disks (lazily created on demand), not all configured disks. It starts empty ('disks=') before any disk() call. List is sorted alphabetically and joined with commas (no spaces). listDrivers similarly emits 'drivers=<sorted-comma-separated-list>' of registered driver names including the built-in 'local'.

trigger_keywords: how is relation_type classified for many-to-any
what fields determine m2a vs m2o relation type
polymorphic relation detection criteria
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: getRelationType returns 'm2a' when: relation.collection === collection AND relation.field === field AND relation.meta?.one_collection_field is truthy AND relation.meta?.one_allowed_collections is truthy. Returns 'm2o' when: relation.collection === collection AND relation.field === field (without the meta polymorphic markers). Returns 'o2m' when: relation.related_collection === collection AND relation.meta?.one_field === field. Returns null otherwise.

trigger_keywords: how does delete handle missing files
what is returned when deleting a non-existent file
delete operation on absent file behavior
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deleting a missing file does NOT throw an error. It returns { wasDeleted: false }. The dispatcher emits 'was_deleted=false'. Deleting an existing file returns { wasDeleted: true } and emits 'was_deleted=true'. The file-not-found condition on delete is silently absorbed.

trigger_keywords: what does prepend do when file does not exist
prepend to non-existent file behavior
how is prepend implemented for missing files
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When prepend is called on a path that does not yet exist, the FileNotFound error is caught internally and the content is written as a new file (equivalent to put). This means prepend creates the file if missing rather than failing. Append, by contrast, uses fse.appendFile which also creates the file if missing.

trigger_keywords: how does parse_json prevent prototype pollution
what key is stripped by safe JSON parser
parse_json behavior with __proto__ property
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: parseJSON strips any literal '__proto__' key from the parsed object. Input '{"a":1,"__proto__":{"polluted":true}}' produces '{"a":1}' (the __proto__ key is dropped). All other keys parse normally. The safe parser re-serializes the result as standard JSON without the stripped key.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.actual_meaning: packages/drive — contains Storage abstract class, StorageManager, LocalFileSystemStorage, and the normalized exception hierarchy (MethodNotSupported, InvalidConfig, DriverNotSupported, FileNotFound, etc.)

packages/shared/src/utils — contains abbreviateNumber, toArray, pluralize, depluralize, getSimpleHash, moveInArray, deepMap, getCollectionType, getEndpoint, getRelationType, isDynamicVariable, parseJSON
getEndpoint() in packages/shared/src/utils/get-endpoint.ts checks collection.startsWith('directus_') and strips the first 9 characters to form the top-level path

context_pointers.vague_reference: special placeholder variables that begin with reserved tokens like the current-time marker
context_pointers.actual_meaning: isDynamicVariable() in packages/shared/src/utils/is-dynamic-variable.ts checks against the array ['$NOW', '$CURRENT_USER', '$CURRENT_ROLE']

context_pointers.vague_reference: storage area so no caller can escape it
context_pointers.actual_meaning: LocalFileSystemStorage._fullPath() uses join(this.$root, join(sep, relativePath)) to clamp all paths inside the configured root, preventing directory traversal

oracle_data.fallback_response: The question does not match any specific constraint. Please ask about one of: unsupported operation error format, manager configuration errors (missing default, missing disk config, missing driver, unregistered driver, duplicate disk), local filesystem operations (path containment, delete of missing file, prepend to missing file, file-not-found error format), utility functions (abbreviateNumber thresholds and unit defaults, getSimpleHash algorithm and output for 'hello', isDynamicVariable recognized prefixes, getEndpoint system prefix, getRelationType m2a/m2o/o2m criteria, parseJSON __proto__ stripping), or registry introspection output format.

feature1_unsupported_driver_operations.description: A storage backend exposes a fixed set of file operations (append, copy, delete, driver introspection, existence check, read-as-string, read-as-buffer, signed-url, stat, read-stream, public-url, move, create/write, prepend, and flat listing). A backend that does not implement a given operation must reject any attempt to use it with a normalized error rather than silently doing nothing. The error is reported as a neutral category line, a stable domain error code, an HTTP-style status, and a human-readable detail that names both the attempted operation and the backend label. The request supplies the operation to probe and a label identifying the backend.

cases.expected_output:
error=method_not_supported
code=E_METHOD_NOT_SUPPORTED
status=500
detail=Method append is not supported for the driver CustomDriver

feature2_1_disk_resolution_errors.description: The storage manager resolves a named disk from a configuration object into a usable storage backend. Resolution fails fast with a normalized error when the configuration is incomplete or references an unknown backend kind. The request supplies the manager configuration (an optional default disk name and a map of disk definitions) and asks the manager to resolve a disk (using the default when no explicit name is given). The covered failures are: no default disk name available, a referenced disk has no configuration entry, a disk entry omits its backend kind, and a disk entry names a backend kind that has not been registered. Configuration errors carry a stable config error code and a status of 500; an unregistered backend kind reports a distinct category with a status of 400 and echoes the offending kind.

cases.expected_output:
error=invalid_config
code=E_INVALID_CONFIG
status=500
detail=Make sure to define a default disk name inside config file

error=driver_not_supported
status=400
driver=foo
detail=Driver foo is not supported

feature2_2_disk_instantiation.description: When a disk is fully and validly configured, the manager instantiates the corresponding backend and returns it, reporting the backend kind that was used. The manager caches instantiated disks: resolving the same disk name more than once returns the very same backend instance rather than constructing a new one each time. The request supplies a valid configuration and asks the manager to resolve a disk and then to confirm that two successive resolutions of the same name yield an identical instance.

feature2_3_custom_driver.description: The manager is extensible: a caller can register a new backend kind under a chosen name, after which disks configured to use that kind resolve to the freshly registered backend. The request registers a custom backend kind and then resolves a disk whose configuration references that kind; the reported backend kind for the resolved disk must be the registered one (not the built-in default).

feature2_4_dynamic_disk.description: Disks can be added to the manager dynamically after construction. Adding a disk under a fresh name makes it resolvable like any configured disk. Adding a disk under a name that already exists is rejected with a normalized configuration error (stable config code, status 500) and the new definition is not applied. The request seeds an initial configuration, then either adds a brand-new disk and resolves it, or attempts to add a disk under an already-used name.

cases.expected_output:
error=invalid_config
code=E_INVALID_CONFIG
status=500
detail=A disk named local is already defined

feature2_5_registry_introspection.description: The manager can be introspected for the set of disks that have actually been instantiated so far and for the set of backend kinds that are registered. The set of instantiated disks starts empty and grows only as disks are resolved on demand (lazy instantiation), reported as a sorted, comma-separated list of disk names. The set of registered backend kinds always includes the built-in default kind plus any kinds registered by the caller, reported as a sorted, comma-separated list. The request issues a sequence of resolutions and/or registrations interleaved with introspection requests.

cases.expected_output:
disks=
disk=local driver=local
disks=local
disk=home driver=local
disks=home,local

feature3_1_write_read_exists.description: The local filesystem backend writes and reads files relative to a root directory, transparently creating any intermediate directories, and reports whether a path currently exists. The request drives a fresh, empty root through a sequence of steps: write a file then read it back (the read returns the exact stored content); write a file in a nested path that does not yet exist (intermediate directories are created on the fly); check existence of a path that was written (true); and check existence of a path that was never written (false).

feature3_2_mutation.description: The local filesystem backend supports mutating operations: delete, move (rename), copy, append, and prepend. Deleting an existing file reports that a deletion occurred and the file no longer exists afterwards; deleting a missing file reports that no deletion occurred (rather than failing). Moving relocates a file so the new path exists and the old path no longer does; copying duplicates a file so both the source and destination exist. Appending adds content to the end of an existing file; prepending adds content to the front. Prepending to a path that does not yet exist creates it with just the given content. The request drives a fresh, empty root through one such scenario per case.

feature3_3_path_containment.description: The local filesystem backend confines every path to its configured root directory so that callers cannot escape it. A leading absolute separator is treated as relative to the root rather than the system root. Leading parent-directory segments that would climb above the root are neutralized (clamped to the root). Parent-directory segments that stay within the root are honored normally. Each case writes content to a potentially-dangerous path and then reads it back from the safe in-root location it actually resolved to, confirming the content round-trips and stayed inside the root.

feature3_4_stream_metadata.description: Beyond reading whole-file string content, the local filesystem backend exposes the same data as a byte buffer and as a readable stream, and reports file metadata. Reading a file as a buffer yields the raw bytes (a binary buffer whose length equals the stored byte count). Reading a file as a stream yields the same content as a flowing sequence of chunks that, concatenated, reproduce the stored text. A file can also be written by piping the readable stream of another existing file into it, copying its content across. Requesting metadata for an existing file reports its size in bytes and a last-modified timestamp. Each case drives a fresh, empty root.

feature3_5_not_found.description: When the local filesystem backend is asked to read a file that does not exist, the underlying operating-system error is normalized into a stable, host-language-neutral file-not-found error rather than surfacing a raw OS exception. The normalized error carries a stable error category, a stable error code, an HTTP-style status, and echoes back the requested path. The request drives a fresh, empty root and attempts to read a path that was never written.

feature3_6_flat_listing.description: The local filesystem backend can enumerate every file under the root as a flat list of root-relative paths, optionally filtered by a path prefix. With an empty prefix it returns all files anywhere beneath the root, recursing into nested directories. A prefix restricts the listing to paths that begin with that prefix; the prefix may match a partial path segment, not just whole directory names. A prefix that matches nothing (including one pointing outside any existing content) yields an empty list. An empty root yields an empty list. Each case drives a fresh, empty root, optionally writes some files, then lists them; the resulting paths are reported as a sorted, comma-separated list.

feature4_abbreviate_number.description: A number formatter renders large numbers in a compact, human-readable form by scaling to thousands, millions, billions, and trillions and appending the matching unit suffix. Numbers below one thousand are returned unchanged (as their plain string form). Numbers at or above one thousand are divided down to the largest fitting magnitude and suffixed (thousands, millions, and so on). The formatter accepts an optional number of decimal places to keep (default zero, i.e. rounded to a whole number) and an optional custom list of unit suffixes to use instead of the defaults. Negative numbers keep their sign. Rounding that pushes a value up to the next magnitude rolls over to the next larger unit.

feature5_to_array.description: A coercion helper guarantees an array result from a value that may or may not already be one. A comma-separated string is split on commas into its individual pieces. A value that is already an array is returned as-is. Any other single value is wrapped in a one-element array. The request supplies one value to normalize, and the resulting array is reported as JSON.

feature6_pluralize.description: A pair of simple string helpers convert between a singular word and its plural form. Pluralizing appends a trailing 's' to the given word. Depluralizing removes the final character of the given word, recovering the singular form. The request supplies one word and the chosen direction; the transformed word is reported.

feature7_simple_hash.description: A lightweight, non-cryptographic hash helper turns an arbitrary string into a short hexadecimal fingerprint. The same input always yields the same fingerprint, and different inputs generally yield different fingerprints. The request supplies one string and the resulting hexadecimal hash is reported.

feature8_move_in_array.description: An array helper relocates a single element from one position to another, returning the reordered array. Moving an element to a later position shifts the intervening elements back toward the source; moving it to an earlier position shifts them forward; moving it to its own position leaves the order unchanged. The request supplies an array plus the source and destination indices, and the resulting array is reported as JSON.

feature9_deep_map.description: A recursive object mapper applies a transform to every leaf value of a (possibly deeply nested) plain object, preserving the original key structure and nesting. Nested objects are traversed in depth, and each scalar leaf is replaced by the transform's result while the surrounding shape is left intact. In these cases the transform stringifies each leaf and prepends a fixed prefix to it. The request supplies the object and the prefix, and the transformed object is reported as JSON.

feature10_collection_type.description: A classifier inspects a collection definition and reports its kind. A collection that carries a database-level schema is a concrete table. A collection that has no schema but does carry metadata is an alias (a purely virtual grouping). A collection with neither schema nor metadata is unknown. The request supplies a collection definition object and the classification is reported.

feature11_endpoint.description: A routing helper maps a collection name to its REST endpoint path. System collections, identified by a reserved name prefix, map to a top-level path named after the part of the collection following that prefix. All other (user-defined) collections map to a path under the generic items route, qualified by the collection name. The request supplies a collection name and the endpoint path is reported.

feature12_relation_type.description: A relational classifier determines how a given collection/field viewpoint participates in a relation definition. When the viewpoint matches the owning side of the relation and the relation declares a polymorphic target (a collection-selector field plus a set of allowed collections), it is a many-to-any. When the viewpoint matches the owning side without those polymorphic markers, it is a many-to-one. When the viewpoint instead matches the related side and names the relation's reverse field, it is a one-to-many. The request supplies a relation definition together with the collection and field forming the viewpoint, and the relationship kind is reported.

feature13_is_dynamic_variable.description: A predicate decides whether a value is a dynamic-variable reference: a string that begins with one of a fixed set of reserved special-variable prefixes (such as the current timestamp, current user, or current role placeholders). Strings starting with such a prefix are dynamic; any other string, or any non-string value, is not. The request supplies one value and the boolean result is reported.

feature14_parse_json.description: A safe JSON parser turns a JSON text into its value, but defends against prototype-pollution by dropping any '__proto__' property encountered during parsing. Ordinary JSON objects, arrays, and scalars parse exactly as standard JSON would; only a literal '__proto__' key is stripped from the result so it cannot tamper with the object prototype. The request supplies a JSON text and the parsed value is reported back as re-serialized JSON.
